content.claim: Structural factors such as residential segregation, neighborhood-level demographic clustering, and long-standing school admission policies are identified by education research as likely primary drivers of between-school segregation in Hungary, with evidence suggesting education policymakers may need radical reforms beyond track structure changes to foster integration.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Polish system reform lens
limitations: Identifies structural drivers but does not quantify their relative causal weights compared to 6/8-year track availability
Implies but does not directly test whether eliminating tracks would substantially reduce segregation if other structural factors remain unchanged
Does not specify which 'radical' policy alternatives have been tested or their measurable outcomes
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
